facts_disclosed: Daughter Sophie lives in New Zealand — Christchurch

turns.content: I have a question about technology, and I suspect you will find it rather basic. I am trying to make a video call and the thing simply will not cooperate. The camera appears to be working but there is no sound at the other end. I am using an iPad, if that is relevant.

Not basic at all — audio issues on video calls are one of the most common problems people run into, and they can be surprisingly tricky to pin down.

Let me walk you through a few things to check:

1. **Is the iPad muted?** Look at the side of your iPad — there's a small switch above the volume buttons. If you see a tiny orange dot, the mute switch is on. Flip it the other way.

2. **Check the volume level.** Press the volume-up button on the side of the iPad and make sure it's turned up. A volume indicator should appear on screen.

3. **Which app are you using for the video call?** FaceTime, Zoom, WhatsApp, or something else? The fix can depend on the app.

4. **Has the app been given permission to use the microphone?** Sometimes iPads block microphone access for specific apps. You can check this in Settings > Privacy & Security > Microphone.

Could you tell me which app you're trying to use? That will help me narrow things down.

It is FaceTime. My daughter Sophie is in Christchurch, in New Zealand, and we speak on Sundays. She arranged the whole thing — set up the iPad, showed me which buttons to press. But today the sound seems to have vanished on her end. She can see me but cannot hear a word I am saying, which is rather frustrating for both of us.

Ah, that's a very specific and solvable problem. If Sophie can see you but not hear you, the issue is with your microphone rather than your speakers. Good news — this is almost always a settings or software glitch rather than a hardware fault.

Let's try these steps in order:

1. **Check the mute button within FaceTime itself.** During the call, look at the bottom of the screen — there should be a row of icons. One looks like a microphone. If it has a line through it, you've accidentally muted yourself. Tap it to unmute.

2. **If that's not it, try ending the call and restarting FaceTime.** Close the app completely — swipe up from the bottom of the screen and swipe the FaceTime window upward to dismiss it. Then reopen FaceTime and call Sophie again.

3. **If it persists, restart the iPad.** Hold down the top button and either volume button until you see the "slide to power off" slider. Turn it off, wait ten seconds, then turn it back on.

The most common cause by far is number 1 — accidentally tapping the mute button during the call. It's very easy to do with a stray finger.

Would you like to try these while Sophie waits?
Oh. I think you may be right about the mute button. I can see a little microphone icon and it does appear to have a line through it. Let me tap it. One moment.
Take your time — give it a tap and see if Sophie can hear you again. I'll be right here if you need further help.
Yes, that has done it. She can hear me now. I feel rather foolish. I must have touched it by accident when I was adjusting the iPad on the table. Sophie is laughing at me, which I suppose I deserve.

Please don't feel foolish — this happens to people of all ages and technical abilities, honestly. The mute button on FaceTime is positioned right where your fingers naturally rest, and it only takes the lightest touch to toggle it. Even seasoned tech users accidentally mute themselves in meetings regularly.

I'm glad it was a quick fix! Now you know what to look for next time — if Sophie suddenly can't hear you, check for that line through the microphone icon first.

A small tip: if you're finding the iPad slippery on the table, you might consider getting a simple stand or case that props it up at an angle. That way your hands are free and you're less likely to accidentally tap something on screen. Sophie might be able to recommend one.

Is there anything else I can help with while you have your call?

A stand is not a bad idea, actually. I have been leaning it against a stack of books, which is not ideal. The time difference makes things a little complicated, too. She is thirteen hours ahead, so our Sunday morning is her Sunday evening. I do try not to keep her too long, but we do not get to talk as often as I would like, being on opposite sides of the world.

Thirteen hours is quite a gap — it sounds like you've found a sensible window with Sunday mornings for you and Sunday evenings for her. That's actually one of the better overlaps for the UK and New Zealand time zones.

For what it's worth, if you ever wanted to add a second call during the week, another good window would be your early evening (around 6–7pm UK time), which would be her morning (7–8am NZ time). It depends on her schedule, of course, but it's worth knowing the option is there.

As for the stand — a basic iPad stand is quite inexpensive, usually around ten to fifteen pounds. Look for one that's adjustable so you can set the angle for your seating position. Something like the Lamicall brand is popular and sturdy. If Sophie does any online shopping for you, she could probably have one sent to your door.

I'm glad we got the audio sorted. Enjoy the rest of your call with Sophie.

Thank you, I shall look into the stand. And I appreciate you not making me feel too hopeless about the mute button. Sophie has just told me to write down the steps you gave me, which is probably sensible. I shall do that after we ring off.

That's a very good idea — a small cheat sheet by the iPad with a few notes can save a lot of frustration next time. You could jot down:

- **No sound?** Check the microphone icon at the bottom of the screen. If there's a line through it, tap it.
- **Still no sound?** Close FaceTime and reopen it.
- **Still nothing?** Restart the iPad (hold top button + volume button).

Simple, clear steps you can glance at if anything goes wrong.

You're far from hopeless — you're keeping in regular contact with your daughter on the other side of the world via video call, which is no small thing. Enjoy the rest of your Sunday, and do come back any time the technology gives you trouble. That's what I'm here for.
Very kind, thank you. I shall write those down now. Good morning.
Good morning! Give my best to Sophie — well, in spirit, at least. Enjoy the rest of your weekend.
